title: Willow garden trug
subtitle: An oval willow basket with two fixed handles for carrying cut flowers and produce
excerpt: A garden trug is a shallow oval basket with a fixed handle at each end, traditionally used for gathering cut flowers and lightweight produce. The oval base and two-handle structure are the main differences from a simple round basket, and both require handling a different staking pattern. A second basketry project after a round basket.
sourceNotes: Oval garden basket and trug forms documented in British horticultural and basketry traditions; Victorian gardening handbooks and Museum of English Rural Life collections.

content.text: An oval basket is different from a round one in the base. The
has more sticks running the long way than the short way. The staking pattern at the
needs to allow for the tighter curve at each end. The
are the same as on a round basket. The oval shape makes this a step up from the first round basket.

attrs.body: Brown willow: soak overnight in cold water. Buff willow: soak one hour. White willow: soak twenty to thirty minutes, then mellow in a damp cloth for several hours. The handle bows need to be thick rods soaked for several hours to bend without cracking; soak handle-bow rods separately and for longer.
attrs.title: Prepare your willows the day before

content.text: For a trug approximately 35 cm long and 22 cm wide: 10 base sticks (4 short, 6 long), 20 stakes (6 long for the ends, 14 medium for the sides), 30 to 40 weavers (fine), and 2 thick rods for the handle bows (at least 90 cm long). All in buff or brown willow.
for inserting stakes and tucking ends, and a rapping iron. No other tools needed.

Cut 4 short base sticks to 25 cm and 6 long sticks to 40 cm. Pierce the 4 short sticks through the centre and thread the 6 long sticks through them, distributing evenly. Bind the slath with two rounds of pairing weave. Open the sticks out to radiate as in a round base, but maintain the 2:3 ratio of short to long sticks by keeping the end sticks further apart (wider spacing at the ends maintains the oval shape). Continue pairing until the base is 30 to 32 cm in the long direction and 18 to 20 cm in the short direction.
Insert 20 stakes, pointed and pushed alongside each base stick into the base weave. At the curved ends of the oval, use the longer stakes; at the straight sides, shorter stakes will do. Work the
for two rounds to turn the stakes upright.

to about 10 cm height. A garden trug is relatively shallow; the sides rise steeply at the ends and more gently at the long sides. Control the splay by adjusting weaver tension at the curved ends: tighter weaving at the ends keeps the oval shape rather than allowing it to round out to a circle.
Finish with two rounds of three-rod waling to lock the side weave, then work a trac border or a three-rod border to close the stake ends.

A trug has two fixed handle bows, one at each end. Point one end of each heavy rod and push it down through the weave at the border on one short end; bend it into an arch and push the other end down at the same short end on the other side. The handle bow should arch about 15 to 18 cm above the basket rim. Wrap the bow at the base on each side with fine willow rods twisted tightly around the bow and tucked into the border weave with the bodkin. Repeat at the other end.
Trim all ends, check that all stake tips are tucked securely, and leave to dry for three days. The trug will stiffen as it dries. No finish is applied.
Use a single central handle bow instead of two end handles. Push the bow down through the border on each long side at the midpoint.
Make the sides taller (15 cm) for a general-purpose shopping basket; the technique is identical, just more rounds of randing.

attrs.intro: Problems with the oval shape and handle bows.

items.fix: Check the base proportions after every four or five pairing rounds and adjust stick spacing before continuing. An oval that loses its shape at the base cannot be recovered later.
items.cause: The end stakes were spaced too close together, or weaving tension pulled the long sides inward to even them.
items.symptom: The oval base gradually becomes round rather than maintaining its shape

items.fix: Remove the bow carefully. Soak the cracked rod for another two hours and try again at a slightly wider arch; a wider arch puts less stress on the bend. If the crack is severe, use a fresh rod.
items.cause: The rod was not soaked long enough to be fully flexible.
items.symptom: The handle bow cracks when bent to the arch shape

items.fix: Dampen the wrapping and pull each coil tight with the bodkin before it dries. A damp wrapping tightens further as it dries.
items.cause: Wrapping rods were not tension-pulled tightly enough at each turn.
items.symptom: The handle wrapping looks loose and slides on the bow

attrs.heading: Common problems
